title: Article 6 of Paris Agreement and Carbon Markets
focus: Mechanisms under Article 6 (6.2, 6.4, 6.8), the role of the National Designated Authority (NDA), and differences between Kyoto Protocol and Paris Agreement carbon trading.
rationale: Article 6 mechanisms are highly examinable for UPSC, especially the distinction between market (6.2, 6.4) and non-market (6.8) approaches, and the transition from Kyoto's CDM to Paris's PACM.
sectionExcerpt: Article 6 enables countries to meet climate goals through voluntary cooperation by establishing international carbon compliance markets. Article 6.2 is a decentralized approach for bilateral cooperation trading ITMOs. Article 6.4 is a centralized Paris Agreement Crediting Mechanism (PACM) succeeding Kyoto's CDM. Article 6.8 introduces non-market approaches. The NDA, a 21-member committee headed by the Environment Ministry Secretary, recommends eligible projects like green hydrogen and CCUS. Unlike Kyoto which limited participation to developed countries, Paris includes all countries and allocates 5 percent of Article 6.4 proceeds to the Global Adaptation Fund.
evidenceBullets: NDA is a 21-member committee headed by the Secretary, Environment Ministry, authorizing projects under Article 6.4.
Article 6.2 involves decentralized bilateral trading of Internationally Transferred Mitigation Outcomes (ITMOs) with corresponding NDC adjustments.
Article 6.4 establishes a centralized global carbon market (PACM), succeeding the Kyoto Protocol's Clean Development Mechanism (CDM).
Article 6.8 focuses on non-market approaches with no trading of emission reductions.
Paris Agreement allocates 5% of proceeds from Article 6.4 transactions to the Global Adaptation Fund, whereas Kyoto directed CDM proceeds to the Adaptation Fund.
Paris Agreement restricts legacy credit use to post-2013 credits, unlike Kyoto which allowed older credits.

title: Montreal Protocol and Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)
focus: Key provisions of the Montreal Protocol, Kigali Amendment, characteristics of HFCs, and India's achievements in phasing out Ozone Depleting Substances.
rationale: The Montreal Protocol and Kigali Amendment are classic UPSC topics. The distinction between ODS and non-ODS (like HFCs), and the specific recovery timelines are prime targets for objective questions.
sectionExcerpt: The Montreal Protocol (signed 1987, implemented under the 1985 Vienna Convention) is a legally binding treaty to eliminate Ozone Depleting Substances (ODS) like CFCs and Halons. The 2016 Kigali Amendment aims to phase down HFCs, which are non-ODS but potent greenhouse gases. HFCs are short-lived climate pollutants with an average atmospheric lifetime of 15 years, warming the planet 3,790 times as much as CO2 over 20 years. The ozone layer is expected to recover to 1980 values by 2040 globally, 2045 over the Arctic, and 2066 over the Antarctic. India phased out CFCs, CTC, and halons by 2010, ahead of schedule.
evidenceBullets: Montreal Protocol is implemented under the Vienna Convention (adopted in 1985).
Kigali Amendment (2016) targets the phase-down of HFCs, which are non-ODS but potent greenhouse gases.
Ozone layer recovery to 1980 values is expected by 2040 globally, 2045 over the Arctic, and 2066 over the Antarctic.
HFCs are synthetic gases used for cooling, with an average atmospheric lifetime of 15 years, and warm the planet 3,790 times as much as CO2 over a 20-year period.
India phased out CFCs, Carbon Tetrachloride (CTC), and halons for controlled use by 2010, ahead of Montreal Protocol schedules.

title: Net Zero Framework for Global Shipping
focus: IMO's Net-Zero Framework, MARPOL Annex VI, and mandatory compliance measures for international shipping.
rationale: International maritime emissions and IMO regulations are emerging environmental topics. The specific thresholds and mechanisms (Global Fuel Standard, MARPOL) are highly testable.
sectionExcerpt: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) approved the Net-Zero Framework for Global Shipping, aiming for net-zero emissions close to 2050. Shipping accounts for almost 3 percent of global GHG emissions. The Framework will be included in Annex VI of MARPOL (International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships). It is the first framework to combine mandatory emissions limits and GHG pricing across an entire sector. It applies to large ocean-going ships over 5,000 gross tonnage (emitting 85 percent of international shipping CO2). Compliance requires adhering to a Global Fuel Standard to reduce greenhouse gas fuel intensity (GFI) and a Global Economic Measure involving remedial units and financial rewards.
evidenceBullets: The Net-Zero Framework for Global Shipping was approved by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) aiming for net-zero by around 2050.
The framework will be included in Annex VI of MARPOL, which prevents air pollution from ships.
Shipping accounts for almost 3% of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.
The framework becomes mandatory for large ocean-going ships over 5,000 gross tonnage.
Ships must comply with a Global Fuel Standard to reduce their annual greenhouse gas fuel intensity (GFI).
An IMO Net-Zero Fund will be established to collect pricing contributions from emissions.
